title: Woven bookmark on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A slim warp-striped band in cotton warp and wool weft
excerpt: Set up a narrow warp of two contrasting cotton colours, weave a plain tabby, and finish the ends with a twisted fringe to make a set of bookmarks. Each bookmark is about 3 cm wide and 20 cm long. The project is a good introduction to rigid-heddle warping without committing to a full-width project.
sourceNotes: Plain-weave bookmark weaving is a standard introductory project in UK rigid-heddle weaving workshops, used to teach warping, sett calculation, and finishing without complex techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: The threads threaded through the heddle and stretched on the loom from front to back. The warp sets the width, sett, and length of the woven piece.
The number of warp threads per centimetre or per inch. A rigid-heddle loom uses a fixed sett reed; 7.5 dpi or 10 dpi are the most common for cotton-warp bookmarks.
The simplest weave structure: the weft passes over one warp thread and under the next, alternating on each row. Also called plain weave. All rigid-heddle weaving in neutral position is tabby.
The combined heddle-and-reed in a rigid-heddle loom. Alternating threads pass through holes and slots; raising the heddle opens one shed, lowering it opens the other.

content.text: Bookmarks are an ideal first project on a rigid-heddle loom. The narrow
takes under 10 minutes to set up, the
weave is straightforward, and the finished pieces are immediately usable. This pattern makes three bookmarks from a single warp, each in a slightly different weft colour combination.

items.name: Wool or cotton weft yarn, 3-4 colours
Rigid-heddle loom, minimum 10 cm weaving width

content.text: of 10 dpi. Thread 24 warp threads for a 6 cm warp width (this will weave off to about 5 cm finished after take-up). Thread 18 ends of colour A and 6 ends of colour B in a stripe sequence: 3A, 6B, 9A, 6B, 3A (or any combination that gives an even total of 24). Thread each end through a hole or slot in the
using the reed hook. Set the warp length to 80 cm for three 20-cm bookmarks plus loom waste.

Weave a hem of two rows with scrap yarn to spread the warp. Begin with the first weft colour: raise the heddle to open the up-shed, pass the stick shuttle through, lower the heddle to open the down-shed, pass the shuttle back. Beat each row gently with the heddle. Change weft colour every 6 to 8 rows for a striped effect, or weave each bookmark in a single colour. Leave 1 cm of unwoven warp between each bookmark for the fringe cut line.

Cut the bookmarks apart at the gaps. Trim the warp tails to an even 3 cm at each end. Twist pairs of adjacent warp threads together tightly and tie an overhand knot at the end to secure the fringe. The twisted fringe is both decorative and functional, preventing the warp from unravelling.

items.problem: The selvedge edges draw in noticeably as weaving progresses
items.cause: The weft is being pulled too tight before beating
items.fix: Leave a slight arc in the weft before beating rather than pulling it straight. A gentle curve gives the weft yarn enough slack to wrap around the outer warp threads without drawing in.

items.problem: The warp stripes are not visible in the finished piece
items.cause: The weft yarn is too thick and is hiding the warp stripe colours
items.fix: Use a finer weft yarn, or weave more loosely so some warp colour shows through. Alternatively, use a warp-dominant structure by beating more firmly to cover the weft.
